one_line_takeaway: An unmodified, official DL compiler can be turned into the backdoor-injection mechanism itself: DcL-BD ships a model that is provably benign and detector-clean before compilation yet reaches ~100% attack success once a standard compiler (TVM/TorchCL/ONNXRuntime/TensorRT/MLIR) optimizes it, exposing floating-point compilation inconsistency as an overlooked ML supply-chain threat.
key_finding: Pre-compilation, DcL-BD models behave like clean models: attack success on triggered inputs (ASR*_M) is at random-guess level (~10% on 10-class CIFAR-10/SVHN, ~1% on 100-class CIFAR-100) and they are rated comparable to CLEAN by four detectors (Neural Cleanse, SCAn, MM-BD, STRIP). After compilation, the trigger achieves ~100% attack success (ASR*_C) across six vision models, three compilers, and two hardware platforms (lowest observed 96.64% for Tiny-RX29 on TorchCL-CPU), while clean accuracy (AccC) is preserved and pre/post-compilation prediction consistency (CR) on clean inputs is ~99.9-100%. The attack transfers across compiler/hardware settings (CPU targets transfer more reliably), is robust to trigger size (4-12) and position, generalizes to TensorRT and MLIR (100% ASR), and extends to NLP (BERT/RoBERTa post-compilation ASR ~0.99 vs ~0.01 clean). Lower FP precision raises ASR (FP32/FP16 > FP64). In the natural setting, 31/100 top HuggingFace models (including a Microsoft ResNet with 220M+ downloads) had reverse-engineerable compilation-induced triggers. Fine-tuning as a defense only partially helps: it drops adversarial ASR (e.g., 100% -> 24.43% for C10-CN) but leaves it well above the clean baseline and does not remove natural triggers.
core_contribution: The paper identifies a new attack surface in the ML stack: unmodified, official deep-learning compilers can silently change a model's semantics during compilation, and this can be weaponized to hide backdoors. Its central original contribution is DcL-BD (Deep learning CompiLer BackDoor), an attack that produces a DL model which is genuinely benign before compilation (the trigger has no effect and it passes SOTA backdoor detectors) but becomes backdoored after an official compiler (TorchCL, TVM, ONNXRuntime, and also TensorRT/MLIR) compiles it for deployment. The exploit leverages floating-point non-associativity: compiler optimizations (e.g., operator fusion) reorder FP operations, introducing tiny numerical deviations that DcL-BD deliberately amplifies through a non-linear activation layer to flip predictions on triggered inputs post-compilation. A second contribution is a preliminary formalization of three compilation-equivalence notions (semantic, decision, observable-decision equivalence) and an empirical demonstration that real compilers achieve only observable decision equivalence, not strict semantic equivalence. A third contribution is an in-the-wild study showing that even without an attacker, compilation can introduce natural triggers in 31 of the top 100 HuggingFace models.
threat_model: Attacker acts as a malicious model provider who trains/fine-tunes a model and publishes it on a public hub (HuggingFace, Model Zoo, GitHub). The victim downloads it, runs security/accuracy checks (which the model passes because it is truly benign pre-compilation), then compiles it with a commercial DL compiler to deploy on mobile/resource-constrained hardware, at which point the backdoor activates. Primary setting is white-box on the compiler: the attacker has access to the same publicly available compiler the victim will use, so they can anticipate compilation effects. The paper also studies a weaker cross-compiler transfer setting where the attacker does not know the victim's exact compiler. Attacker capability is minimal (train/fine-tune a model on public data, publish it); no access to the victim's inputs or inference-time queries is needed. The key false assumption exploited is that compilers are believed to preserve model semantics, and compiled binaries strip high-level information, so the backdoor is hard to inspect.
limitations: The strongest results assume white-box knowledge of the victim's compiler; cross-compiler transfer is real but uneven (ASR varies by source/target setting). The attack's ASR is occasionally below the model-level baseline BELT (e.g., 96.64% vs 100%) because DcL-BD's dual objective (benign pre-compilation, malicious post-compilation) is a harder constraint. The in-the-wild reverse-engineering was run only with TorchCL on GPU using 100 ImageNet seed images, and the recovered natural triggers are large (tens of thousands of pixels changed), so their practical stealth is limited. The paper offers no fully effective defense: fine-tuning is shown insufficient, and existing SMT-based DL-compiler verification (targeting logical bugs in MLIR) does not cover unavoidable floating-point deviations; a numerically-aware verification defense is left as future work. The formal equivalence definitions are only checked empirically over 100 sampled inputs (observable decision equivalence), not proven.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (with ConvNet C10-CN and VGG16 C10-V16)
CIFAR-100 (with ResNet18 C100-R18 and VGG19 C100-V19)
TinyImageNet (with ResNet34 Tiny-R34 and ResNeXt29 Tiny-RX29)
ImageNet (100 seed images for in-the-wild trigger reversal)
Google PoJ104/POJ15 and Yelp (NLP: BERT, RoBERTa)
quantitative_results: Preliminary study: log-max numeric deviation between original and compiled models ranged from about 10^-6 to 10^-12 (Table 1), non-zero in all settings but decision-equivalent on 100 sampled inputs
Pre-compilation attack success rate ASR*_M at random-guess level: ~10% (10-class CIFAR-10), ~1% (100-class CIFAR-100); e.g., C10-CN 10.73% (TorchCL-CPU), Tiny-RX29 0.60%
Post-compilation ASR*_C reaches 100% for most model/compiler/hardware combos; lowest is 96.64% (Tiny-RX29, TorchCL-CPU) and 96.65-96.85% for Tiny-RX29 on GPU/ORT (Table 3)
Clean-input consistency rate CR between pre- and post-compiled models is ~99.9-100% (Table 4); post-compilation clean accuracy AccC preserved (e.g., C10-V16 ~92.76%)
Ablation (Table 8): full DcL-BD gives AccM 87.65, ASR*_M 10.80, AccC 87.65, ASR*_C 100.00; removing any single module collapses either stealth or effectiveness
Generalization (Table 5): TensorRT+Ours and MLIR(iree)+Ours both reach 100% post-compilation ASR with AccC ~88.4-88.5
Targeting a specific setting (Table 6): 99.79% ASR on TorchCL-GPU target while ~10.8% on non-target compilers
NLP (Table 7): post-compilation ASR ~0.99 for BERT/RoBERTa vs ~0.01 (POJ) / ~0.17-0.19 (Yelp) for CLEAN

In-the-wild: 31 of 100 top HuggingFace models had reverse-engineerable compiler-induced triggers (threshold ASR 0.8); example Microsoft ResNet with 220M+ downloads; 25,373 features removed in one case

Fine-tuning defense (Table 9): adversarial ASR drops from 100% to 24.43% (C10-CN), 100% to 18.39% (C10-V16), 99.99% to 4.78% (C100-R18) but stays above clean baseline; natural triggers persist (88/98/126 triggers found post-fine-tuning)
baselines_compared: Backdoor detectors used as evaluators: Neural Cleanse, SCAn, MM-BD, STRIP
Compilers compared: TorchCL (Torch Compiler), Apache TVM 0.9.0, OnnxRuntime 1.20.0, plus TensorRT and MLIR(iree) for generalization
Hardware: Intel Xeon E5-2650 v4 CPU and Nvidia RTX 6000 GPU
